title: The diverging lens: one image, always virtual
scenario: A **diverging** lens (concave — thinner in the middle) with focal length $f = -1$ m. The focal length is *negative* because parallel rays leave it **spreading apart**, as if they had come from a focus *in front* of the lens. An object $h_o = 0.4$ m tall stands a distance $d_o$ in front. Here is the surprise: no matter where you put the object — right up close or far away — a diverging lens makes only **one** kind of image: **virtual, upright, and shrunken**, on the same side as the object. Slide the object and watch — the image never flips, never enlarges, never lands on a screen. This is the lens in a door peephole and the front group of a wide-angle view.

assumptions.claim: A *thin* lens: its thickness is negligible next to the focal length, so both refractions happen in one plane at the lens center.
*Paraxial* rays — all rays stay close to the axis, so a point object maps to a single point image. Real wide lenses blur this.
A single diverging lens, $f < 0$, with the sign convention that a virtual image (on the object's side, seen through the lens) has $d_i < 0$ and an upright image has $m > 0$.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (two rules)
steps.prose: Two memorized equations. The first locates the image; the second gives its size and orientation. A negative $m$ means the image is inverted; $|m| < 1$ means reduced; a negative $d_i$ means the image is virtual (on the object's side).

steps.label: Solve the lens equation for the image distance
steps.prose: With $f = -1$ m and $d_o = 1.5$ m, the image distance is $d_i = -0.6$ m. It is **negative**, so the image is **virtual** — it forms on the *same* side as the object. You cannot catch it on a screen; you see it by looking *through* the lens.

steps.label: Read off the magnification and image height
steps.prose: The magnification is $m = 0.4$: the sign is positive, so the image is **upright**, and $|m| < 1$, so it is **reduced** — a diverging lens does this at *every* object distance — the image is always virtual, upright, and shrunken, and can never be projected.

image_distance.label: Image distance $d_i = d_o f/(d_o-f)$ at $d_o = 1.5$ m

image_height.label: Image height $h_i = m\,h_o$ at $d_o = 1.5$ m, $h_o = 0.4$ m

steps.label: Draw three rays from the tip of the object — no equation needed
steps.prose: Geometric optics needs no calculus and, at first, no algebra. From the object tip, the ray *parallel* to the axis bends **outward**, as if it had come from the near focus $F$; the ray through the lens *center* goes straight; the ray aimed at the far focus $F'$ leaves *parallel*. The refracted rays diverge — but traced *backward* they meet, and that crossing is the (virtual) image.

steps.label: Two of the rays give the magnification two different ways (similar triangles)
steps.prose: The center ray makes two similar triangles (object–axis–center and image–axis–center), so $h_i/h_o = -d_i/d_o$. The parallel ray makes two more about a focal point, so $h_i/h_o = -(d_i - f)/f$. Same ratio $h_i/h_o$, computed from two different rays.

steps.label: Set the two equal — and the thin-lens equation falls out
steps.prose: Demanding that the two rays cross at one point — that the two magnifications agree — and clearing fractions gives $1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i$ exactly. The equation you memorized is the algebra of *the rays meet*. (Deeper still, Fermat's principle: every path takes a stationary time, $dL = 0$ — the one genuinely calculus statement underneath it all.)

proof.heading: The thin-lens equation is the algebra of “the three principal-ray constructions locate one image” — SymPy proves the chief, parallel, and focal rays all agree.
proof.detail: check $d_i = d_o f/(d_o-f)$ solves $1/d_o+1/d_i=1/f$; chief ray $m=-d_i/d_o$; parallel & focal rays give the same $m$; $m = f/(f-d_o)$

checks.claim: The closed form $d_i = \dfrac{d_o f}{d_o - f}$ satisfies the thin-lens equation $\dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ — back-substituting leaves zero.
The ray through the lens *center* is undeviated, so the object and image triangles about the center are similar: $m = h_i/h_o = -d_i/d_o$.

The *parallel* ray refracts toward (or away from) the focus, giving $m = -(d_i - f)/f$ by similar triangles. It equals the chief-ray value $-d_i/d_o$ — the two independent constructions locate the *same* image point, and that agreement is exactly the thin-lens equation.
The *focal* ray (through a focus, emerging parallel) gives $m = -f/(d_o - f)$ — the same magnification again. All three principal-ray constructions locate one image point.
The magnification in terms of $d_o, f$ alone: $m = -\dfrac{d_i}{d_o} = \dfrac{f}{f - d_o}$ — its sign sets the orientation (upright when $m>0$, inverted when $m<0$), its size the scale.

misconception.claim: If you move the object close enough to a diverging lens, you can magnify it or catch a real image on a screen.
misconception.refuted_by: You cannot. A diverging lens has no real focus in front of it — parallel rays *spread*, and only their backward extensions meet, at a virtual focus *behind* the object. So for **every** object distance the image distance $d_i = d_o f/(d_o - f)$ comes out **negative** (virtual) and the magnification $|m| < 1$ (reduced and upright). Drag the object from $0.3$ m to $4$ m: the image only ever shrinks toward the focal point, always right-side-up, never on the far side. Only a converging lens ($f > 0$) can magnify or project.

practice.prompt: A diverging lens has focal length $f = -1$ m. An object sits at $d_o = 1.5$ m. Where is the image — what is $d_i$?

choices.misconception: Dropped the minus sign. A diverging lens always makes a **virtual** image, so $d_i$ is **negative** — the image is on the same side as the object, not the far side.
Treated the lens as converging ($f = +1$). A diverging lens has $f < 0$; with $f = -1$ m, $d_i = d_o f/(d_o - f)$ works out negative, not positive.

From the object tip, the ray *parallel* to the axis bends **outward**, as if it had come from the near focus $F$; the ray through the lens *center* goes straight; the ray aimed at the far focus $F'$ leaves *parallel*. The refracted rays diverge — but traced *backward* they meet, and that crossing is the (virtual) image.

calculus_steps.prose: The center ray makes two similar triangles (object–axis–center and image–axis–center), so $h_i/h_o = -d_i/d_o$. The parallel ray makes two more about a focal point, so $h_i/h_o = -(d_i - f)/f$. Same ratio $h_i/h_o$, computed from two different rays.

practice.prompt: The same diverging lens ($f = -1$ m) with the object at $d_o = 1.5$ m forms its image at $d_i = -0.6$ m. What is the magnification $m$?

choices.misconception: Made it negative. A diverging-lens image is **upright**, so $m > 0$. (A negative $m$ would mean an inverted image — that only happens for a *real* image from a converging lens.)
Used $d_o + f$ in the denominator. The magnification is $m = -f/(d_o - f)$ — with $f = -1$ that is $1/2.5 = 0.4$, a reduced upright image.
